learning_objectives: Classify cybercrime by type, motivation, and impact using established taxonomies
Distinguish between computer-as-target, computer-as-tool, and computer-as-incidental crimes
Identify the actors, techniques, and objectives in major cybercrime categories
Apply the Cyber Kill Chain framework to analyze cybercrime incidents
readings: Brenner, Cybercrime: Criminal Threats from Cyberspace, Ch. 1-3
FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) Annual Report
Europol Internet Organised Crime Threat Assessment (IOCTA)

lecture_notes.introduction: Cybercrime investigation combines the rigor of traditional criminal investigation with the technical complexity of digital forensics. Before we can investigate cybercrime, we must understand what cybercrime is, how it is categorized, and how the categories determine investigation strategy. Cybercrime is not a single type of offense but a broad spectrum ranging from financially motivated fraud to state-sponsored espionage, from individual harassment to attacks on critical infrastructure. Each category involves different actors, techniques, evidence sources, and legal frameworks. The FBI's IC3 received over 880,000 complaints in 2023, with losses exceeding $12.5 billion, and these figures represent only reported crimes. Understanding the typology of cybercrime shapes every aspect of the investigation: where to look for evidence, what legal authorities apply, which agencies to coordinate with, and what technical skills are needed. A business email compromise investigation requires financial tracing and email analysis. A ransomware investigation requires malware analysis and cryptocurrency tracking. A child exploitation investigation requires image forensics and undercover operations. This course builds your ability to investigate all of these categories using proper legal procedures and forensically sound methods.
key_concepts: Cybercrime categories: financial crime (BEC, wire fraud, card fraud), intellectual property theft, espionage (state and corporate), hacktivism, cyberterrorism, exploitation offenses, identity theft, and cyberstalking
Computer-as-target crimes: hacking, denial of service, malware deployment, unauthorized access (18 USC 1030 CFAA)
Computer-as-tool crimes: fraud, identity theft, exploitation where the computer is the instrument but not the target
Computer-as-incidental crimes: traditional crimes where the computer holds evidence (drug trafficking communications, murder planning)
Actor categorization: script kiddies, organized criminal groups, nation-state actors, insider threats, hacktivists, and lone wolf actors
Cyber Kill Chain: reconnaissance, weaponization, delivery, exploitation, installation, command and control, actions on objectives
Financial impact: BEC accounts for the largest dollar losses, ransomware for the most operational disruption, exploitation for the most investigative resources
Trend analysis: cryptocurrency-enabled crime, AI-generated fraud, supply chain attacks, and ransomware-as-a-service models
Investigation strategy by crime type: how the category determines evidence sources, legal process, and inter-agency coordination
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three real cybercrime indictments (publicly available DOJ press releases) to identify the crime type, the evidence chain that led to charges, the legal authorities used, and the investigative techniques referenced. Map each case to the Cyber Kill Chain phases to show how the investigation traced the attack from actions on objectives back to the attacker.
setup_requirements: DOJ cybercrime indictment documents (3 selected cases)
Cyber Kill Chain mapping template
IC3 Annual Report for statistics
Jurisdiction analysis worksheet

review.value: A BEC investigation focuses on email headers, domain registration, and financial tracing. A network intrusion investigation focuses on malware analysis, C2 infrastructure, and log analysis. A cryptocurrency fraud investigation focuses on blockchain analysis and exchange records. Always identify the crime type first to determine your investigative approach.

The Kill Chain framework helps investigators work backwards from the observed impact. If you detect actions on objectives (data exfiltration), trace back to C2 communications, then to the initial exploitation vector, then to the delivery mechanism, and ultimately to the attacker's infrastructure and identity.

IC3 data significantly underestimates actual cybercrime because most victims never report. Businesses fear reputational damage, individuals feel embarrassed, and many victims do not realize they have been victimized. Actual losses are estimated at 3-5 times reported figures.

FBI handles most federal cybercrime, Secret Service handles financial cybercrime and critical infrastructure, HSI handles transnational cybercrime, and state/local agencies handle crimes within their jurisdiction. Large investigations often involve multiple agencies, requiring clear deconfliction.

learning_objectives: Define digital evidence and its unique characteristics compared to physical evidence
Apply the Federal Rules of Evidence to digital evidence authentication and admissibility
Identify volatile and non-volatile evidence sources and their order of collection priority
Evaluate the legal standards for digital evidence in criminal proceedings
readings: NIST SP 800-86 Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response
Federal Rules of Evidence: Rules 401, 402, 403, 702, 901, 1001-1008
Casey, Digital Evidence and Computer Crime, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital evidence is information stored or transmitted in digital form that has probative value in a legal proceeding. It is simultaneously the most abundant and the most fragile form of evidence available to investigators. A single smartphone may contain more evidence than an entire warehouse of paper documents: call records, text messages, GPS locations, photos with metadata, browsing history, app data, and deleted files recoverable through forensic analysis. However, digital evidence is easily altered, accidentally destroyed, and technically complex to authenticate. The legal system has adapted its rules to accommodate digital evidence, but the fundamental requirements remain: relevance, authenticity, and reliability. An investigator who collects compelling digital evidence but fails to maintain chain of custody, uses unreliable tools, or cannot explain the evidence to a jury will see that evidence excluded or discredited. This session establishes the foundation for all digital evidence handling throughout the course: what makes evidence admissible, how to maintain its integrity, and the hierarchy of evidence collection that guides investigative priorities.
key_concepts: Digital evidence characteristics: easily copied without degradation, potentially volatile, metadata-rich, large in volume, requires specialized tools to analyze, and can be remotely destroyed

Order of volatility (RFC 3227): CPU registers and cache, routing tables and ARP cache, process memory, temporary files, disk data, remote logging data, physical configuration, archival media; collect most volatile first
Evidence admissibility requirements: relevance (FRE 401-402), not unduly prejudicial (FRE 403), authenticated (FRE 901), best evidence rule for digital records (FRE 1001-1008)

Authentication of digital evidence under FRE 901(b)(4): distinctive characteristics, internal patterns, hash verification, and chain of custody documentation establish that the evidence is what it claims to be
Best evidence rule and digital copies: under FRE 1003, a forensic image verified by hash is treated as equivalent to the original because digital copies are exact duplicates

Hearsay considerations: computer-generated records (logs, timestamps) are generally not hearsay because they are not human statements; computer-stored records (emails, documents) may require hearsay exceptions
Expert testimony requirements under Daubert: forensic methodology must be testable, peer-reviewed, have a known error rate, and be generally accepted
Types of digital evidence: direct evidence (confession in an email), circumstantial evidence (IP address linking suspect to crime), corroborating evidence (timestamps consistent with other evidence)
Evidence preservation orders: legal mechanisms to prevent destruction of digital evidence before it can be collected through proper legal process
lecture_notes.demonstration: Examining a sample evidence package from a mock investigation. Review the forensic image acquisition report, verify hash values, examine the chain of custody documentation, and identify gaps that a defense attorney could exploit. Then analyze three types of digital evidence (email, log file, deleted file) and assess the admissibility requirements for each.
setup_requirements: Sample evidence package with forensic image report
Hash verification tools (md5sum, sha256sum)
Chain of custody form template
Federal Rules of Evidence reference

review.value: A cryptographic hash (SHA-256) of the original media and the forensic image must match exactly. This proves the image is a bit-for-bit copy. Calculate the hash at acquisition time, document it in the acquisition report, and verify it before analysis. Any discrepancy means the evidence may be challenged.

Memory forensics must happen before shutting down a system. Live RAM contains encryption keys, running processes, network connections, and malware that exists only in memory. Pulling the power plug destroys all volatile evidence. Always capture a memory image first if the system is running.

Authentication proves the evidence is what you claim it is (hash values, metadata analysis). Chain of custody proves the evidence has not been tampered with since collection (documented transfer log). Both are required: a properly authenticated image with a broken chain of custody may be challenged.

Web server logs are computer-generated (not hearsay) because they record events without human input. Emails are computer-stored (potentially hearsay) because they contain human statements. This distinction affects admissibility: computer-stored records may need a hearsay exception like the business records rule (FRE 803(6)).

learning_objectives: Maintain a forensically sound chain of custody from seizure through trial
Document evidence handling procedures that satisfy court requirements
Identify common chain of custody failures and their legal consequences
Implement digital chain of custody tracking for large-scale investigations
readings: DOJ Searching and Seizing Computers and Obtaining Electronic Evidence in Criminal Investigations, Ch. 3
SWGDE Best Practices for Collection of Digital and Multimedia Evidence
NIST SP 800-86 Appendix: Evidence Collection Forms

lecture_notes.introduction: Chain of custody is the chronological documentation showing the seizure, custody, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition of evidence. In digital forensics, chain of custody must account for both the physical media (hard drives, phones, USB devices) and the digital copies (forensic images, extracted data) created during analysis. A broken chain of custody does not automatically exclude evidence, but it gives the defense a powerful argument that the evidence may have been altered, contaminated, or planted. Courts evaluate chain of custody challenges by considering whether the gap or irregularity created a reasonable possibility of tampering. In practice, chain of custody failures have derailed major cybercrime prosecutions. Evidence stored in unlocked rooms, transfer logs with missing entries, forensic images created without hash verification, and analysis conducted on original media rather than copies have all resulted in successful defense challenges. This session covers the procedural requirements for maintaining an unbroken chain of custody in cybercrime investigations, from the moment evidence is first identified through its presentation in court. We will use the SWGDE (Scientific Working Group on Digital Evidence) standards as our framework, which are recognized by federal courts and accreditation bodies.
key_concepts: Chain of custody elements: who collected it, when, where, how it was stored, who had access, every transfer between custodians, and the current location
Physical evidence handling: evidence bags with tamper-evident seals, unique evidence numbers, photographs of devices before and during collection, and secure storage
Digital evidence chain: forensic image creation with hash verification, write-blocker usage documented, analysis performed on copies never on originals, hash re-verification before testimony
Evidence tracking systems: barcode-based tracking, RFID-tagged evidence containers, digital chain of custody software (Evidence.com, SAFE)
Multi-jurisdictional considerations: transferring evidence between agencies requires documented handoffs, and different agencies may have different chain of custody requirements
Cloud evidence challenges: data changes continuously, there is no physical media to seize, and the chain of custody must document how the data was preserved and collected from the cloud provider
Write-blockers: hardware devices that prevent any modification to the original media during imaging; the use of a write-blocker must be documented in the acquisition report
Laboratory evidence procedures: evidence intake, secure storage (locked evidence room with access logs), check-out/check-in procedures, and evidence return or destruction

Common failures: unsigned transfer forms, missing time entries, evidence left on an analyst's desk overnight, analysis on original media, and hash verification performed only after the defense requests it
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete chain of custody exercise. Seize a simulated evidence hard drive: photograph it in place, assign an evidence number, complete the seizure form, package it in a tamper-evident bag, transport it to the lab, log it into the evidence management system, connect it through a write-blocker, create a forensic image with hash verification, and transfer the image to the analysis workstation with documented chain of custody at every step. Then deliberately introduce a chain of custody gap and discuss how a defense attorney would exploit it.
setup_requirements: Sample hard drive for evidence handling exercise
Write-blocker (hardware or software)
Evidence bags and tamper-evident seals
Chain of custody forms and evidence log templates
Forensic imaging software (FTK Imager or dc3dd)

review.value: A write-blocker prevents any writes to the evidence media during imaging. Without a documented write-blocker, the defense can argue that the imaging process itself modified the evidence. Software write-blockers are acceptable but hardware write-blockers are preferred because they are independent of the operating system.

Calculate and verify the hash every time evidence is transferred: at acquisition, at lab intake, before analysis, and before testimony. If any hash check fails, the entire evidence chain is compromised. Use SHA-256 (not MD5 alone) because MD5 collision attacks are practical.

For cloud evidence obtained via legal process (subpoena to a provider), the chain of custody starts when the provider produces the data. Document: the legal process used, the provider's certification of authenticity, the hash of the received data, and secure storage from receipt forward. The provider's internal chain of custody is their responsibility.

Defense attorneys look for: unsigned forms, time gaps in the custody log, evidence stored in unsecured locations, analysis performed on originals, missing hash documentation, and any person in the chain who cannot be called to testify. Anticipate these challenges and document preemptively.

learning_objectives: Create forensically sound disk images using multiple acquisition methods
Select the appropriate imaging format and method for different evidence types
Verify image integrity through cryptographic hash comparison
Handle acquisition challenges including encrypted drives, damaged media, and live systems
readings: Nelson, Guide to Computer Forensics and Investigations, Ch. 4-5
SWGDE Best Practices for Disk Imaging
NIST Computer Forensics Tool Testing Program reports

lecture_notes.introduction: Forensic imaging is the process of creating an exact bit-for-bit copy of digital storage media for analysis. The image is the foundation of the entire investigation: if the image is flawed, incomplete, or unverifiable, every analysis result built upon it is questionable. The imaging process must be performed using validated tools, with write protection to prevent modification of the original, and with cryptographic hash verification to prove the copy is exact. Different evidence types require different acquisition methods. A powered-off hard drive can be removed and imaged with a hardware write-blocker. A running server that cannot be shut down requires a live acquisition that captures the drive contents while the system remains operational. An encrypted drive may require a live acquisition to capture the decrypted contents from memory before the system is powered off. A RAID array requires understanding of the RAID configuration to reconstruct the logical volume from individual disks. Mobile devices have their own acquisition methods ranging from logical extraction (files and databases) to physical extraction (raw NAND dump). This session covers the tools, techniques, and decision-making process for forensic acquisition across all common evidence types.
key_concepts: Bit-for-bit imaging: copying every sector of the source media including unallocated space, slack space, and deleted file remnants; this captures data that file-level copying would miss
Image formats: raw/dd (exact copy, largest size), E01/EnCase (compressed, with metadata and built-in hash), AFF4 (open format with compression and encryption support)
Hardware write-blockers: dedicated devices that physically prevent write commands from reaching the evidence media; must be tested and validated per NIST CFTT standards
Software write-blockers: OS-level write protection; acceptable when hardware write-blockers are unavailable, but less defensible in court
Live acquisition: imaging a running system through a forensic agent or bootable USB; necessary when the system cannot be shut down or when encryption would lock the data
Memory acquisition: capturing the contents of RAM for volatile evidence analysis; tools include WinPMEM, AVML (Linux), and DumpIt
Encrypted drive handling: if the drive is decrypted (system running), perform a live logical acquisition to capture decrypted content; capture memory first to preserve encryption keys
RAID reconstruction: documenting RAID level, stripe size, and disk order before disassembling the array; imaging each disk individually and reconstructing logically
Verification: calculating SHA-256 hash of source and image simultaneously during acquisition; any mismatch indicates a failed acquisition that must be repeated
lecture_notes.demonstration: Perform three types of forensic acquisition. First, dead acquisition: remove a hard drive, connect through a hardware write-blocker, image with dc3dd, and verify hashes. Second, live acquisition: use FTK Imager to image a running Windows system, capturing both the logical drive and physical memory. Third, handle an encrypted BitLocker volume by first capturing memory (to preserve the encryption key), then performing a logical acquisition of the decrypted volume. Document each acquisition with proper hash verification and chain of custody.
setup_requirements: Forensic workstation with dc3dd and FTK Imager
Hardware write-blocker (Tableau or equivalent)
Evidence hard drives (2 prepared drives for imaging)
Windows VM with BitLocker for encrypted acquisition exercise
WinPMEM for memory acquisition

review.value: Dead acquisition (system powered off) is the gold standard because the evidence cannot change during imaging. Live acquisition is necessary when: the system cannot be shut down (critical server), full-disk encryption would lock the data, or volatile evidence (memory) must be captured. Live acquisition introduces risks of evidence changes during imaging.

E01 (EnCase) format includes compression (typically 50-70% size reduction), built-in hash verification, case metadata, and segmented files. Raw/dd is the simplest format with universal tool compatibility. E01 is preferred for long-term storage; raw is preferred for tools that require direct disk access.

If a hash mismatch occurs, the acquisition failed. Common causes: bad sectors on the source (use dc3dd's error handling to log and skip), intermittent cable connection, hardware write-blocker issue, or insufficient destination storage. Document the failure, resolve the cause, and re-image completely.

Mobile forensics uses different acquisition methods: logical (files and databases via backup or agent), file system (full file system access with root/jailbreak), and physical (raw NAND dump). Each level captures more data but requires more invasive access. Tool selection depends on device model, OS version, and security configuration.

learning_objectives: Analyze NTFS, ext4, and APFS file systems for forensic artifacts
Recover deleted files from unallocated space and file slack
Interpret file system metadata including timestamps, permissions, and alternate data streams
Detect anti-forensic techniques including timestomping and file wiping
readings: Carrier, File System Forensic Analysis, Ch. 8-14 (NTFS sections)
Ext4 file system forensics reference
SANS DFIR poster: Windows Forensic Analysis

lecture_notes.introduction: Every file on a computer exists within a file system that records not just the file contents but extensive metadata about creation, modification, access, ownership, and deletion. For forensic investigators, file system analysis is where abstract digital evidence becomes concrete: we can determine when a file was created, who created it, when it was last accessed, whether it was renamed or moved, and even recover files that the user deleted. NTFS, the primary Windows file system, is particularly rich in forensic artifacts. The Master File Table (MFT) records metadata for every file and directory, including files that have been deleted. NTFS timestamps record four distinct times (creation, modification, access, and MFT entry modification) that reveal the history of file operations. Alternate Data Streams allow data to be hidden within existing files. The $LogFile and $UsnJrnl record file system transactions that survive deletion and overwriting. Understanding these artifacts and their reliability is essential for building accurate timelines and detecting anti-forensic manipulation. This session covers NTFS forensics in depth, with comparative coverage of ext4 (Linux) and APFS (macOS) file systems.
key_concepts: NTFS Master File Table (MFT): metadata record for every file including filename, timestamps, size, permissions, and data location; MFT entries persist after deletion until overwritten
NTFS timestamps: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (SI) timestamps are easily modified by user-space tools; $FILE_NAME (FN) timestamps require kernel-level access to modify and are more reliable
Deleted file recovery: when a file is deleted, the MFT entry is marked as available but not overwritten; the file data remains in unallocated space until new data overwrites those clusters
File slack and RAM slack: the space between the end of file data and the end of the allocated cluster may contain data from previously deleted files or from memory
NTFS Alternate Data Streams (ADS): named data streams attached to files that are not visible in standard directory listings; used by malware to hide executables
NTFS $LogFile: transactional journal recording file system operations; useful for determining what operations occurred even when the file system appears clean
NTFS $UsnJrnl (Update Sequence Number Journal): records changes to files and directories with timestamps; survives file deletion and is invaluable for timeline analysis
Timestomping detection: comparing $STANDARD_INFORMATION timestamps with $FILE_NAME timestamps; if SI shows a 2020 creation date but FN shows 2024, the timestamp was manipulated
Ext4 forensics: inode-based metadata, journal recovery, extents for file data location, and deleted file recovery from orphan inodes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a forensic image containing evidence of cybercrime. Use Autopsy or Sleuth Kit to examine the NTFS file system: recover deleted files from unallocated space, identify timestomped files by comparing SI and FN timestamps, discover hidden data in alternate data streams, and reconstruct a timeline of file system activity using $UsnJrnl entries. Show how the recovered and hidden files, combined with the timeline, build the investigative narrative.
setup_requirements: Autopsy or Sleuth Kit forensic analysis software
Forensic image containing planted evidence (deleted files, timestomped files, ADS)
Timeline analysis tools (log2timeline/Plaso)
NTFS artifact reference guide

review.value: When a file is deleted, its MFT entry is marked as unused but the metadata (filename, timestamps, data cluster addresses) remains until a new file reuses that MFT entry. On a half-full drive, deleted file metadata may persist for weeks or months. This is why forensic imaging captures the entire drive including unallocated space.

Compare $STANDARD_INFORMATION and $FILE_NAME timestamps. SI timestamps can be modified with user-space tools (SetMACE, Timestomp). FN timestamps require kernel-level access and are rarely modified by attackers. If SI creation date is 2020 but FN creation date is 2024, the file was created in 2024 and the timestamps were manipulated.

NTFS allows multiple data streams per file. The default (unnamed) stream contains the visible file content. Named streams (file.txt:hidden_stream) can contain arbitrary data including executables. Use 'dir /R' or Streams.exe to detect ADS. Malware frequently uses ADS to hide payloads within innocent-looking files.

The $UsnJrnl records file creation, deletion, rename, and modification events with timestamps. Even if a file is deleted and overwritten, the journal entry recording its creation and deletion may survive. Parse with MFTECmd or usnjrnl_dump to build a comprehensive timeline of file activity.

learning_objectives: Extract and analyze Windows registry artifacts relevant to cybercrime investigations
Interpret Windows event logs for authentication, execution, and lateral movement evidence
Recover browser history, USB device connections, and application execution artifacts
Build forensic timelines from multiple Windows artifact sources
readings: Carvey, Windows Forensic Analysis Toolkit, Ch. 3-7
SANS Windows Forensic Analysis poster
Microsoft Windows Event Log reference documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Windows is the dominant operating system in enterprise environments and on most personal computers, making Windows artifact analysis the most commonly needed forensic skill. Windows records an extraordinary amount of information about user activity, application execution, network connections, and system events across dozens of artifact sources. The Windows Registry alone contains thousands of forensically relevant entries: recently accessed files, USB devices ever connected, network shares accessed, applications installed, user accounts, and system configuration. Windows Event Logs record authentication successes and failures, process creation, network connections, scheduled task execution, and service installations. Browser artifacts reveal web history, downloads, bookmarks, and cached credentials. Each artifact source has known limitations and anti-forensic weaknesses that investigators must understand to assess reliability. This session covers the key Windows artifacts for cybercrime investigation, how to extract and interpret them, and how to combine multiple artifact sources into a coherent investigative timeline that is resilient to anti-forensic manipulation because no single artifact source can be altered without leaving traces in others.
key_concepts: Windows Registry forensics: SAM (user accounts and password hashes), SYSTEM (system configuration, network interfaces, USB device history), SOFTWARE (installed applications, network connections), NTUSER.DAT (per-user: recent documents, run history, typed URLs)
ShellBags: registry entries recording folder access, including folders on USB devices and network shares; persist after the folder or device is removed
UserAssist: registry keys recording GUI program execution with execution count and last run timestamp; encoded with ROT-13
Prefetch files: created when executables run, recording the executable path, run count, last 8 run timestamps, and files accessed during the first 10 seconds of execution
Windows Event Logs: Security log (4624/4625 logon success/failure, 4688 process creation), System log (service installations, driver loads), PowerShell log (script execution)
SRUM (System Resource Usage Monitor): records per-application network usage, CPU time, and energy consumption for the past 30 days; identifies applications that transferred large amounts of data
Browser artifacts: history (SQLite databases), downloads, bookmarks, cached credentials, session cookies, and IndexedDB storage
USB device history: SYSTEM\ControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR records device serial numbers, first and last connection timestamps, and assigned drive letters
Timeline analysis: combining multiple artifact sources (MFT, Event Logs, Prefetch, Registry, Browser) into a unified chronological view using tools like log2timeline/Plaso
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a Windows forensic image from a suspected insider data theft case. Extract and interpret: Registry artifacts showing USB device connections and recently accessed files, Prefetch files showing execution of file compression and cloud upload tools, Event Logs showing after-hours logon events, SRUM data showing large network transfers, and Browser history showing access to personal cloud storage. Combine all artifacts into a Plaso timeline that tells the story of the data theft from initial access to exfiltration.
setup_requirements: Windows forensic image (prepared with insider threat scenario)
Registry Explorer or RegRipper for registry analysis
Eric Zimmerman tools (PECmd, MFTECmd, ShellBagsExplorer, TimelineExplorer)
Plaso/log2timeline for timeline generation
Autopsy for integrated analysis

review.value: Prefetch files prove that an executable was run, how many times, and the last 8 timestamps. If a suspect claims they never ran a data exfiltration tool but C:\Windows\Prefetch\WINRAR.EXE-12345678.pf exists with a last run time during the theft period, that is strong evidence. Prefetch survives application uninstallation.

Security Event 4624 (successful logon) combined with Event 4688 (process creation with command line logging enabled) reveals who logged in, when, and what they executed. Cross-reference with 4663 (file access auditing) to show what files they accessed. This combination reconstructs the suspect's entire session.

SRUM records bytes sent and received per application. If Dropbox.exe shows 2 GB sent at 2 AM on the date of the alleged theft, that is powerful evidence. SRUM data covers 30 days and is stored in a database that standard anti-forensic tools do not target.

Sophisticated suspects may clear event logs (but Event 1102 records the clearing), delete prefetch files (but MFT records the deletion), or use private browsing (but DNS cache and SRUM still record network activity). No single artifact tells the whole story, but the combination of multiple artifacts is extremely resilient to tampering.

learning_objectives: Acquire and analyze volatile memory from Windows and Linux systems
Identify running and hidden processes, network connections, and injected code in memory dumps
Extract encryption keys, passwords, and other sensitive data from memory
Detect fileless malware and rootkits that exist only in memory
readings: Ligh et al., The Art of Memory Forensics, Ch. 1-6
Volatility 3 documentation and command reference
SANS Memory Forensics cheat sheet

lecture_notes.introduction: Memory forensics examines the contents of a computer's volatile memory (RAM) to find evidence that exists nowhere else. When a system is running, RAM contains the complete state of every process, network connection, encryption key, typed password, and injected malware. Shutting down the system destroys all of this evidence. Modern attackers increasingly use fileless malware that never touches the disk, residing entirely in memory to evade traditional antivirus and disk-based forensics. Memory-only malware injects into legitimate processes, uses PowerShell and WMI for persistence, and leaves no artifacts on the file system. Memory forensics is the only way to detect and analyze these threats. Beyond malware analysis, memory forensics recovers encryption keys (allowing decryption of full-disk encryption), passwords and session tokens, clipboard contents, chat messages, and recently viewed documents. The Volatility framework is the industry-standard open-source tool for memory analysis, providing plugins for process enumeration, network connection listing, DLL analysis, code injection detection, and hundreds of other analysis capabilities. This session covers memory acquisition techniques, Volatility analysis methodology, and the specific artifacts that memory forensics reveals for cybercrime investigation.
key_concepts: Memory acquisition: tools like WinPMEM, AVML, and DumpIt capture the contents of physical RAM to a file; acquisition must happen before power-off
Volatility 3 framework: open-source memory analysis tool with plugins for Windows, Linux, and macOS memory analysis
Process analysis: listing running processes (pslist), detecting hidden processes (psscan, psxview), identifying parent-child relationships (pstree), and examining process memory for injected code
Network connections: extracting active TCP connections and listening ports from memory (netscan), revealing C2 communications that network logs may have missed
Code injection detection: comparing in-memory code sections against on-disk executables to identify injected shellcode, hooked functions, and modified system libraries
Credential extraction: using plugins like hashdump (SAM hashes), lsadump (LSA secrets), and mimikatz-style extraction of cleartext passwords from LSASS process memory
Encryption key recovery: extracting AES keys from memory for BitLocker, VeraCrypt, and other full-disk encryption tools; must be done before the system is powered off
Fileless malware indicators: PowerShell processes with large memory regions, WMI event subscriptions, reflectively loaded DLLs, and process hollowing (legitimate process with injected malicious code)

Memory analysis methodology: start with process listing, check for anomalies (unexpected processes, hidden processes), examine network connections, check for injection, and extract artifacts relevant to the investigation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a memory dump from a system compromised with fileless malware. Using Volatility 3: list processes to identify suspicious entries (PowerShell with unusual parent process), scan for hidden processes, examine network connections to identify C2 communication, detect code injection in a legitimate process (svchost.exe), extract the injected shellcode, and recover credentials from LSASS memory. Document each finding with the Volatility command used and the forensic significance of the result.
setup_requirements: Volatility 3 installed on analysis workstation
Memory dump from compromised Windows system (prepared scenario)
Volatility symbol tables for the target Windows version
YARA rules for malware signature scanning in memory

review.value: Process hollowing replaces the code of a legitimate process with malicious code. The process appears normal in task manager but its memory contents differ from the on-disk executable. Volatility's malfind plugin compares in-memory sections against disk, revealing injected or replaced code in processes like svchost.exe or explorer.exe.

LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service) stores authentication credentials in memory. In Windows versions prior to Credential Guard, cleartext passwords may be present. Even with Credential Guard, NTLM hashes are available. This is the same technique used by Mimikatz and by attackers performing pass-the-hash attacks.

Memory is a snapshot: it shows the system state at acquisition time but not historical activity. Processes that terminated before acquisition are gone (unless fragments remain). Memory dumps can be very large (16-128 GB) and analysis is resource-intensive. False positives require careful verification.

Sophisticated malware may detect memory acquisition attempts and self-destruct, encrypt its memory regions, or manipulate kernel structures to hide from analysis tools. Cross-referencing multiple Volatility plugins (pslist vs psscan vs psxview) helps detect such evasion because each plugin uses different kernel structures.

learning_objectives: Capture and analyze network traffic for forensic evidence of cybercrime
Reconstruct network sessions to identify data exfiltration and C2 communication
Trace attackers through proxy chains and VPN infrastructure using network evidence
Correlate network forensic evidence with host-based artifacts
readings: Davidoff and Ham, Network Forensics: Tracking Hackers through Cyberspace, Ch. 6-12
Wireshark network forensics reference
SANS Network Forensic Analysis poster

lecture_notes.introduction: Network forensics analyzes network traffic to detect, investigate, and prove criminal activity that traverses computer networks. While host-based forensics tells you what happened on a specific computer, network forensics reveals communications between computers: who talked to whom, when, what protocols were used, and in some cases the content of the communication. Network evidence is critical for cybercrime investigation because almost all cybercrimes involve network communication: malware phones home to command and control servers, exfiltrated data is transmitted over the network, and attackers access compromised systems remotely. Network evidence exists in two forms: full packet capture (the complete content of every network packet) and flow data (metadata about network connections without content). Full packet capture provides the richest evidence but requires massive storage. Flow data is more practical for long-term retention and pattern analysis. This session covers the tools and techniques for capturing, analyzing, and presenting network forensic evidence. We focus on the investigative methodology: starting from an alert or indicator, tracing the network activity backward to identify the source, and forward to identify the impact, building a complete picture of the criminal activity that can be corroborated with host-based evidence.
key_concepts: Full packet capture: recording every byte of network traffic; provides complete evidence including content but requires enormous storage (1 Gbps link generates about 10 TB per day)
NetFlow/IPFIX: metadata about network connections (source IP, destination IP, ports, protocol, bytes, timestamps) without content; enables long-term pattern analysis with manageable storage
Wireshark analysis: filtering, following TCP streams, extracting files from HTTP/FTP sessions, and analyzing DNS queries
DNS forensics: DNS query logs reveal every domain accessed, even for encrypted HTTPS connections; DNS tunneling used for covert C2 and data exfiltration
C2 communication detection: identifying beaconing patterns (regular interval connections to the same host), long-lived connections, and anomalous protocols on standard ports

Data exfiltration indicators: large outbound transfers (especially to new destinations), DNS tunneling (encoded data in DNS queries), HTTPS to unusual destinations, and encrypted traffic on non-standard ports
IP attribution challenges: VPNs, Tor, proxy chains, and compromised machines used as pivot points all obscure the attacker's true IP address
WHOIS and hosting provider analysis: tracing IP addresses to hosting providers and using legal process to obtain subscriber information

Packet capture as evidence: captured traffic must be stored with hash verification and chain of custody to be admissible; the capture system must be validated and the analyst must explain the capture methodology
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a packet capture from a network intrusion investigation. Using Wireshark and Zeek: identify the initial exploitation vector (phishing email with malicious link), trace the malware download from the C2 server, detect beaconing activity (regular interval HTTP POST requests with encoded data), identify data exfiltration (large DNS TXT responses indicating DNS tunneling), and extract the exfiltrated data by reassembling the DNS tunnel. Cross-reference network timestamps with Windows Event Log timestamps to build a corroborated timeline.
setup_requirements: Wireshark for packet analysis
Zeek (formerly Bro) for network metadata extraction
NetworkMiner for automated analysis and file extraction
PCAP file containing the simulated intrusion scenario
DNS analysis tools for tunnel detection

review.value: C2 beaconing shows a regular pattern of connections at fixed intervals (e.g., every 60 seconds) to the same destination. Jitter (randomized delays) makes detection harder but statistical analysis still reveals the pattern. Plot connection intervals on a histogram: beaconing shows a peak at the configured interval. Tools like RITA automate beaconing detection.

DNS tunneling encodes data in DNS queries (subdomains) and responses (TXT records). Example: base64-encoded-data.evil.com. Detection: look for unusually long domain names, high volume of DNS queries to a single domain, and TXT record responses with high entropy. Even encrypted connections must use DNS to resolve hostnames.

Network evidence is strongest when corroborated with host evidence. A network capture showing data transferred to IP X at time T, combined with host evidence showing the suspect logged in at time T and ran an upload tool, builds a much stronger case than either source alone.

Capturing network traffic on your own network (as a victim organization) is generally legal under the provider exception. Capturing on someone else's network requires a wiretap order (Title III) for content or a pen register order for metadata. Evidence captured without proper authority may be inadmissible.

learning_objectives: Perform forensic acquisition of iOS and Android devices at logical and physical levels
Extract and analyze location data, messaging artifacts, and application data from mobile devices
Handle mobile device security features including encryption, biometrics, and remote wipe
Evaluate mobile forensic tools and their limitations for different device models and OS versions
readings: Epifani and Stirparo, Learning iOS Forensics, Ch. 1-5
Hoog, Android Forensics, Ch. 3-7
NIST SP 800-101 Rev 1: Guidelines on Mobile Device Forensics

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile devices are the most personal and pervasive digital evidence sources in cybercrime investigations. A smartphone contains a detailed record of its owner's life: communications (calls, texts, email, messaging apps), location history (GPS, cell tower connections, Wi-Fi access points), social relationships (contacts, social media), financial activity (banking apps, payment history), health data, photographs with embedded metadata, and browsing history. For cybercrime investigators, mobile devices may contain evidence of the crime itself (planning communications, phishing messages received), evidence of the suspect's location at the time of the crime (GPS and cell data), evidence of relationships with co-conspirators (messaging and call records), and evidence of financial transactions related to the crime (cryptocurrency wallets, banking apps). However, mobile forensics presents unique challenges. Modern smartphones use full-disk encryption by default. iOS devices require the passcode to decrypt any data. Android devices with file-based encryption similarly protect user data. Biometric authentication (fingerprint, face) adds another layer. Remote wipe capabilities can destroy evidence if the device connects to a network. And the rapid pace of mobile OS updates means forensic tools must constantly adapt to new security features. This session covers the acquisition methods, tools, and analysis techniques specific to mobile device forensics.
key_concepts: Mobile acquisition levels: logical (files and databases via backup or API), file system (full file system access via root/jailbreak or bootloader exploit), physical (raw NAND dump, most data including deleted items)
iOS forensics: iTunes backup extraction (encrypted and unencrypted), advanced logical via checkm8 exploit (older devices), GrayKey and UFED for passcode bypass on supported models
Android forensics: ADB backup extraction, custom recovery for file system access, JTAG and chip-off for physical acquisition of older devices, bootloader exploits for newer devices
Location evidence: GPS coordinates in photos (EXIF), significant locations database (iOS), Google location history, cell tower connection logs, Wi-Fi access point history
Messaging forensics: iMessage/SMS databases (sms.db), WhatsApp (end-to-end encrypted but stored decrypted on device), Signal (encrypted databases), Telegram, and deleted message recovery
Application data analysis: each app stores data in its sandbox directory; social media apps, banking apps, cryptocurrency wallets, and note-taking apps all contain potentially relevant evidence
Cloud data acquisition: iCloud backups (with warrant to Apple), Google account data (with warrant to Google), and third-party cloud app data may contain more evidence than the device itself
Anti-forensic challenges: device encryption, secure enclave (iOS), biometric lockout, remote wipe via MDM or Find My iPhone, and factory reset with varying effectiveness
Legal considerations: compelling biometric unlock (Fifth Amendment issues), border search exception for mobile devices (Riley v. California requires a warrant), and SCA for cloud data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Perform a forensic acquisition and analysis of an Android device. First, establish the device model, OS version, and security configuration. Perform a logical acquisition using ADB backup and a commercial tool (Autopsy/ALEAPP). Extract and analyze: call logs and SMS messages, WhatsApp message database with deleted message recovery, GPS location history correlated with the investigation timeline, installed applications and their data, and browser history including incognito session artifacts. Document findings in an evidence report.
setup_requirements: Android test device with prepared scenario data
ADB (Android Debug Bridge) tools
ALEAPP (Android Logs Events and Protobuf Parser)
Autopsy with mobile forensics modules
Commercial mobile forensic tool (if available)

review.value: Modern iOS (since iPhone 5S) and Android (since Android 10) encrypt user data with keys derived from the user's passcode and hardware-bound keys. Without the passcode, physical acquisition yields only ciphertext. Logical acquisition requires an unlocked device or valid backup password. This is the fundamental challenge of modern mobile forensics.

SQLite databases (used by iMessage, WhatsApp, and most messaging apps) mark deleted records as free space but do not immediately overwrite them. Forensic tools can recover deleted messages by parsing SQLite free pages and WAL (Write-Ahead Log) files. Recovery success depends on database activity since deletion.

A smartphone generates location data from multiple sources: GPS (precise but battery-intensive), Wi-Fi access point scanning (always active), cell tower connections (always connected), and app-specific location logging. Combined, these sources can place the device at a specific location within meters and reconstruct a detailed movement history.

Cloud evidence (iCloud, Google account) may contain more data than the device because it includes historical backups, cross-device synchronization, and data from replaced devices. Legal process to Apple or Google may be more productive than device acquisition, especially if the device is locked.

learning_objectives: Analyze email headers to trace message origin and identify spoofing
Investigate social media accounts using open-source intelligence techniques
Obtain evidence from email and social media providers through legal process
Correlate email and social media evidence with other investigation sources
readings: Email header analysis reference (RFC 5322)
DOJ Guide to Social Media Evidence Collection
Stored Communications Act (18 USC 2703) practical guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Email and social media are primary communication channels for both cybercriminals and their victims. Business email compromise, phishing campaigns, social engineering, harassment, threats, and fraud all leave evidence in email systems and social media platforms. Email headers contain a wealth of investigative information: the true sending server, the authentication results, the relay path, and timestamps that can identify the sender's location and infrastructure. Social media accounts reveal relationships, communications, location check-ins, and behavioral patterns. Both email and social media evidence exist in two locations: the user's device (which may be acquired through device forensics) and the provider's servers (which must be obtained through legal process). The Stored Communications Act (18 USC 2703) governs law enforcement access to stored communications from providers, with different legal thresholds for subscriber information, transactional records, and content. This session covers the technical analysis of email and social media evidence and the legal process required to obtain it from providers.
key_concepts: Email header analysis: Received headers read bottom-to-top show the relay path; the originating IP is in the bottom-most Received header; compare with SPF, DKIM, and DMARC results

Email spoofing detection: SPF failures indicate the sending IP is not authorized for the sender's domain; DKIM failures indicate the message was modified; Display Name spoofing uses a trusted name with a different email address
Phishing email analysis: examining URLs (hover vs actual destination), attachment analysis (file type, embedded macros, payload analysis), and lookalike domains (registration date, WHOIS)
Social media open-source intelligence (OSINT): username search across platforms, metadata in posted images, geolocation from photos and check-ins, relationship mapping from friends/followers

Legal process for email providers: subpoena for subscriber information (name, address, IP logs), 2703(d) court order for transactional records (headers, to/from), search warrant for content (email body, attachments)
Social media preservation: send preservation letters to prevent account deletion, request emergency disclosure if life is at risk (18 USC 2702(c)(4))
Evidence documentation: screenshot social media content with timestamps and URLs before it is deleted; use web archival tools for persistent preservation
Cross-platform correlation: linking the same person across email and social media accounts using shared phone numbers, email addresses, IP addresses, and behavioral patterns
Encrypted messaging challenges: end-to-end encrypted messaging (Signal, WhatsApp) means providers have no content to produce; evidence must come from endpoint devices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Investigate a BEC (Business Email Compromise) case. Analyze the phishing email headers to identify the true origin, examine the lookalike domain (WHOIS registration date, hosting provider), trace the reply-to email account through provider records obtained via legal process, correlate the attacker's IP addresses with social media accounts used for reconnaissance, and map the financial flow from the fraudulent wire transfer through intermediary accounts. Build the complete attribution chain from email header to suspect identity.
setup_requirements: Email header analysis tools (MxToolbox, Google Admin Toolbox)
OSINT tools for social media investigation (Maltego, Holehe, Sherlock)
WHOIS and DNS lookup tools
Sample BEC email with full headers for analysis
Lookalike domain for investigation exercise

review.value: Each mail server that handles a message adds a Received header at the top. Read from bottom to top to trace the message path. The bottom-most Received header contains the originating IP. However, sophisticated attackers may use compromised mail servers, making the originating IP a compromised machine rather than the attacker's actual IP.

Subpoena: basic subscriber info (name, address, IP logs, payment info). No judicial approval needed. 2703(d) Court Order: transactional records (email headers, login timestamps). Requires specific and articulable facts. Search Warrant: email content and attachments. Requires probable cause. Always use the highest authority available to avoid suppression challenges.

Publicly posted social media content can be viewed and documented without legal process. But creating fake accounts to friend/follow a suspect may constitute deception requiring supervisor approval and legal review. Automated scraping may violate platform terms of service. Follow your agency's OSINT policy.

Email and social media providers will preserve account data for 90 days (renewable) upon receiving a preservation letter from law enforcement. Send preservation requests immediately upon identifying relevant accounts, before the formal legal process is complete. This prevents the suspect or provider from deleting evidence.

learning_objectives: Navigate the dark web safely for investigative purposes using Tor and I2P
Identify and de-anonymize dark web actors through operational security failures
Understand legal authorities for dark web investigations including NITs
Preserve evidence from ephemeral dark web content
readings: Bartlett, The Dark Net, selected chapters
DOJ Dark Web Investigation guidelines
FBI Silk Road investigation case study

lecture_notes.introduction: The dark web consists of internet services accessible only through anonymizing networks like Tor and I2P. These networks are designed to conceal the IP addresses of both servers and users, making them attractive for criminal activity. Dark web marketplaces sell drugs, stolen data, malware, hacking services, counterfeit documents, and other contraband. Dark web forums host discussions of criminal techniques, share stolen data dumps, and facilitate criminal networking. Investigating crimes on the dark web requires specialized techniques because traditional network-based attribution does not work. If an attacker operates through Tor, you cannot trace their IP address through network logs alone. Instead, dark web investigations rely on a combination of: operational security failures by the criminal (using the same username on clear and dark web, leaking real IP through misconfigured services), cryptocurrency tracing (following the money from dark web transactions to regulated exchanges), undercover operations (engaging with suspects on dark web forums to gather intelligence), and technical measures (Network Investigative Techniques that deploy code to identify users). This session covers the methodology for safe dark web investigation, the de-anonymization techniques that have succeeded in major cases, and the legal frameworks that authorize these investigative methods.
key_concepts: Tor network architecture: client connects through three relays (guard, middle, exit); each relay knows only the previous and next hop; hidden services use rendezvous points for mutual anonymity
Investigative OPSEC: use a dedicated investigation machine (preferably Tails OS), connect through a VPN before Tor, never log into personal accounts, never reveal your real identity on dark web sites

OPSEC failures as the primary de-anonymization method: Ross Ulbricht (Silk Road) was identified through his early forum posts using his real email, Bitcoin forum posts linked to his name, and Stack Overflow questions from his real account

Cryptocurrency tracing for dark web: Bitcoin transactions are pseudonymous; chain analysis links wallet addresses to identities through exchange KYC records, clustering analysis, and transaction graph analysis

Network Investigative Techniques (NITs): court-authorized deployment of code on dark web servers that identifies connecting users by collecting their real IP address, MAC address, and system information

Dark web OSINT: monitoring forums and marketplaces for threat intelligence, identifying vendor operations through linguistic analysis and product characteristics, and linking dark web personas to clear web identities

Evidence preservation challenges: dark web content is ephemeral (marketplaces go offline, forums are deleted, onion addresses change); capture screenshots, HTML archives, and full page saves immediately
Legal authority: NITs require a Rule 41 warrant; undercover operations require supervisor approval; purchases from dark web markets require agency authorization and proper handling of contraband
Case studies: Silk Road (OPSEC failure), AlphaBay (OPSEC failure and cryptocurrency tracing), Welcome to Video (cryptocurrency tracing and NIT)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze the Silk Road investigation to identify every OPSEC failure that led to Ross Ulbricht's identification and arrest. Map the investigation timeline: the initial Stack Overflow post linking 'altoid' to Ulbricht's real email, the Bitcoin forum posts, the connection to the Silk Road server's real IP through a misconfigured CAPTCHA page, and the undercover operations that gathered evidence. Discuss what Ulbricht could have done differently and what investigation techniques would have been needed if he had maintained better OPSEC.
setup_requirements: Tails OS running in a VM for safe dark web access (demonstration only)
Tor Browser for .onion site navigation
Silk Road case study materials (public court documents)
Blockchain analysis tool for cryptocurrency tracing demonstration
Dark web monitoring reference resources

review.value: The vast majority of dark web arrests result from operational security failures, not from breaking Tor's anonymity. Common failures: reusing usernames across clear and dark web, cryptocurrency linked to real identity through exchange KYC, geolocation metadata in posted images, consistent writing style across personas, and social engineering by undercover agents.

In the Playpen case, the FBI deployed a NIT on a dark web child exploitation site. A single Rule 41 warrant authorized the NIT to identify users connecting from anywhere in the world. Critics argued this exceeded the warrant's jurisdictional authority. Congress amended Rule 41 in 2016 to explicitly authorize remote access warrants for anonymized computer searches.

Bitcoin transactions are recorded on a public blockchain. Chain analysis companies (Chainalysis, Elliptic) cluster wallet addresses to entities and can trace funds through mixers and exchanges. Even privacy coins like Monero may have vulnerabilities. Cryptocurrency tracing has become a primary investigation tool for dark web cases.

Dark web investigation exposes investigators to illegal content, malicious software, and the risk of identity exposure. Always use a dedicated investigation machine that never connects to personal or organizational networks. Never download files directly. Use Tails OS (amnesic, leaves no trace). Document all access for legal defensibility.

learning_objectives: Draft a search warrant affidavit for digital evidence in a cybercrime case
Apply the Fourth Amendment's particularity requirement to digital evidence searches
Navigate the Stored Communications Act for obtaining provider records
Understand mutual legal assistance treaties for international cybercrime evidence
readings: DOJ Searching and Seizing Computers manual, Ch. 1-5
18 USC 2701-2712 (Stored Communications Act)
Carpenter v. United States (2018) decision summary

lecture_notes.introduction: The legal framework for cybercrime investigation is as important as the technical skills. Evidence obtained without proper legal authority is inadmissible, and investigators who exceed their authority may face personal liability and departmental sanctions. The Fourth Amendment prohibits unreasonable searches and seizures and requires warrants to be based on probable cause and to particularly describe the place to be searched and the items to be seized. Applying these 18th-century principles to 21st-century digital evidence creates unique challenges. How do you particularly describe the data to be searched on a smartphone that contains hundreds of thousands of files? When does the Stored Communications Act require a warrant versus a subpoena versus a court order? How do you obtain evidence from a server in another country? The Supreme Court's decision in Carpenter v. United States (2018) extended warrant requirements to cell site location information, signaling a broader trend toward requiring warrants for digital surveillance. This session covers the practical legal procedures that investigators must follow to ensure their evidence is admissible and their investigations are constitutionally sound.
key_concepts: Fourth Amendment requirements: probable cause (facts establishing that evidence of a crime will be found in the place to be searched) and particularity (specific description of place and items)
Particularity for digital evidence: specify the devices, types of data, date ranges, and search methodology; avoid general warrants that authorize searching everything on a device
Two-step search protocol: the warrant authorizes seizure of the physical device, then a separate protocol governs the forensic search of the device's contents within the warrant's scope
Stored Communications Act thresholds: subpoena (subscriber info), 2703(d) court order (transactional records), warrant (content); post-Carpenter, always seek a warrant for content
Mutual Legal Assistance Treaties (MLATs): formal process for obtaining evidence from foreign countries; slow (6-12 months) but legally binding; alternatives include the CLOUD Act

CLOUD Act (2018): allows US law enforcement to compel US-based providers (Google, Microsoft, Apple) to produce data regardless of where it is stored, and creates a framework for bilateral agreements with foreign governments
Consent exceptions: voluntary consent to search does not require a warrant; document consent carefully; the scope of consent can be limited by the consenting party
Exigent circumstances: emergency situations (imminent destruction of evidence, threat to life) may justify warrantless seizure but not warrantless search of contents
Border search exception: limited applicability to digital devices; Riley v. California requires a warrant to search cell phone contents, but the border exception is still debated
lecture_notes.demonstration: Draft a complete search warrant affidavit for a cybercrime case. Starting with a scenario (business email compromise with wire fraud), identify the probable cause facts, describe the digital evidence to be searched with appropriate particularity, specify the search methodology (keywords, date ranges, communication accounts), cite the legal authority, and address anticipated challenges (encryption, cloud storage, multi-device). Review common drafting mistakes that lead to warrant challenges.
setup_requirements: Search warrant affidavit template
BEC case scenario with probable cause facts
Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure Rule 41 reference
Sample approved warrant affidavits (redacted) for comparison

review.value: For a fraud investigation, the warrant might authorize searching for: financial records, emails referencing wire transfers or account numbers, communication with identified co-conspirators, for the date range of the fraud scheme. It should not authorize searching all files on the device without limitation. Overly broad warrants risk suppression of all evidence.

During a lawfully authorized search, evidence of other crimes found in plain view may be seized. If you are searching for fraud evidence and find child exploitation material, you can seize it. But you cannot use the fraud warrant as a pretext to search for other crimes. The search methodology must be designed for the crime specified in the warrant.

MLAT requests go through diplomatic channels and can take 6-12 months. The CLOUD Act allows direct compulsion of US providers regardless of data location, resolving the Microsoft Ireland case. For data held by foreign providers, MLATs remain necessary. For data held by US providers (Google, Microsoft, Apple) serving foreign users, the CLOUD Act is faster.

Carpenter v. United States (2018) held that accessing historical cell site location information constitutes a Fourth Amendment search requiring a warrant. The broader principle: individuals have a reasonable expectation of privacy in the comprehensive record of their physical movements. This reasoning may extend to other forms of pervasive digital surveillance.

learning_objectives: Write a forensic examination report that meets court admissibility standards
Document investigative methodology in sufficient detail for peer review and replication
Organize evidence exhibits for presentation in legal proceedings
Prepare case documentation for prosecution review and grand jury presentation
readings: SWGDE Guidelines for Forensic Examination Reports
DOJ Computer Crime and Intellectual Property Section report templates
Expert witness report writing best practices

lecture_notes.introduction: The forensic examination report is the formal document that communicates your findings, methodology, and conclusions to prosecutors, defense attorneys, judges, and juries. A technically brilliant investigation that is poorly documented may fail in court, while a well-documented investigation with modest findings can succeed. The report must satisfy multiple audiences: prosecutors need to understand the evidence to decide whether to charge and how to present the case; defense attorneys will scrutinize every detail looking for weaknesses; judges must evaluate whether the methodology meets Daubert standards; and juries must understand complex technical findings explained in plain language. Forensic report writing follows strict conventions: clearly separate facts from opinions, document the methodology so it can be replicated, provide precise references to evidence (exhibit numbers, file paths, timestamps), and avoid speculation or advocacy. The report should be objective even when the findings strongly support the prosecution's theory. Your role is to present the facts and your expert analysis, not to argue for conviction. This session covers the structure, content, and writing standards for forensic examination reports, with emphasis on the specific elements that courts and compliance standards require.
key_concepts: Report structure: case information, authorization, evidence received, examination methodology, findings, conclusions, appendices with technical details

Methodology documentation: every tool used (name, version), every analysis step performed, and every decision made must be documented in sufficient detail for another qualified examiner to replicate the analysis
Fact vs opinion separation: findings section reports what was found (fact); conclusions section provides the examiner's expert interpretation (opinion); keep them clearly separated
Evidence referencing: every finding must reference specific evidence by exhibit number, file path within the forensic image, hash value, and relevant timestamps
Chain of custody integration: the report must reference the chain of custody documentation and confirm evidence integrity (hash verification) at each stage
Limitations and caveats: explicitly state what was not examined, what tools could not access (encrypted containers, damaged sectors), and any factors that limit the conclusions
Visual aids: timeline charts, network diagrams, annotated screenshots, and relationship graphs help non-technical audiences understand complex findings

Language standards: avoid jargon where possible, define technical terms when first used, write in clear declarative sentences, avoid hedging language (use 'consistent with' rather than 'seems to suggest')
Peer review: have another qualified examiner review the report before submission for technical accuracy, completeness, and clarity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write a complete forensic examination report for a cybercrime investigation case. Starting from raw analysis notes, organize findings into the standard report format: document the methodology, reference specific evidence for each finding, draw conclusions supported by the evidence, acknowledge limitations, and create visual aids (timeline and network diagram) for the most complex findings. Then review a deliberately flawed report and identify the weaknesses a defense attorney would exploit.
setup_requirements: Forensic report template (SWGDE format)
Analysis notes from a completed mock investigation
Timeline visualization tool
Deliberately flawed report for critical review exercise

review.value: Another qualified forensic examiner, given the same evidence and your report, should be able to reproduce your findings. This means documenting: tool names and versions, search terms used, analysis parameters, and the decision process for what was examined. If you used Autopsy 4.21 with keyword search for 'wire transfer', document exactly that.

The report should state: 'The email records show that an email was sent from account X to account Y at time T containing instructions to transfer $50,000.' Not: 'The suspect sent a fraudulent email directing the victim to transfer money.' The former is a factual finding; the latter is a conclusion that includes legal determinations (fraud, suspect, victim) outside the examiner's expertise.

Explicitly stating limitations (encrypted volume not examined, damaged sectors on source drive, analysis limited to date range specified in warrant) protects you from cross-examination challenges. The defense cannot attack you for not examining something you explicitly stated was outside scope.

The report will be provided to the defense through discovery. Everything in it will be scrutinized. Do not include speculation, personal opinions about the suspect, or information outside the scope of your examination. If case agents told you the suspect confessed, that does not belong in your forensic report.

learning_objectives: Prepare to testify as an expert witness in cybercrime proceedings
Defend forensic methodology and conclusions under cross-examination
Present complex technical evidence to non-technical judges and juries
Understand the Daubert standard for admissibility of expert testimony
readings: Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals (1993) decision
Expert witness testimony guidelines for forensic examiners
Cross-examination preparation for digital forensics experts

lecture_notes.introduction: Expert witness testimony is where the investigation meets the courtroom. As a forensic examiner, you may be called to testify about your analysis, methodology, and conclusions. Unlike fact witnesses who testify about what they observed, expert witnesses are allowed to offer opinions based on their specialized knowledge. This privilege comes with heightened scrutiny: the court must first determine that you are qualified as an expert (based on education, training, experience, and publications), and then determine that your testimony meets the Daubert standard for reliability. Under Daubert, the judge acts as a gatekeeper, evaluating whether your methodology is based on testable principles, has been subject to peer review, has a known error rate, and is generally accepted in the field. Cross-examination by the defense attorney is designed to undermine your credibility, challenge your methodology, and create doubt about your conclusions. Effective preparation means knowing your report inside out, understanding the limitations of your tools and methods, and being able to explain complex technical concepts to a jury of non-experts. This session covers the practical aspects of expert witness preparation: qualifying as an expert, direct examination, cross-examination tactics and responses, and the art of translating technical forensics into courtroom language.
key_concepts: Expert qualification: education (degrees, certifications), training (courses, workshops), experience (years, number of cases), publications, and prior testimony; be prepared to present your CV and answer qualification questions
Daubert standard four factors: (1) testability of the methodology, (2) peer review and publication, (3) known or potential error rate, (4) general acceptance in the relevant scientific community
Direct examination: the attorney who called you leads you through your qualifications, methodology, findings, and conclusions; use this opportunity to educate the jury

Cross-examination tactics: challenging tool reliability, questioning methodology choices, highlighting limitations you did not mention, suggesting alternative explanations, attacking chain of custody, and testing your knowledge of edge cases

Responding to cross-examination: answer only the question asked, do not volunteer information, say 'I don't know' when appropriate, refer to your report for specific details, and never argue with the attorney

Jury communication: use analogies (IP address is like a phone number, not a person), visual aids (timelines, diagrams), and plain language; avoid jargon; focus on what the evidence means, not how you found it

Maintaining objectivity: acknowledge limitations honestly, concede valid points made by the defense, and never appear to be advocating for the prosecution; your credibility depends on being seen as objective

Preparation checklist: re-read your report, review all exhibits, prepare for Daubert challenges to your tools, practice explaining your methodology in simple terms, and review the defense's expert report if available

Common pitfalls: overstating conclusions beyond what the evidence supports, using jargon the jury does not understand, becoming argumentative during cross-examination, and making definitive statements about attribution that cannot be proven
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a mock trial exercise. One student presents digital forensic evidence as an expert witness, explaining the analysis methodology and findings to a mock jury. Another student (or the instructor) conducts cross-examination, challenging the forensic imaging process, the tool reliability, the chain of custody, and the conclusions. The class evaluates the testimony for clarity, accuracy, and persuasiveness. Focus areas: defending hash verification methodology against claims of hash collision, explaining why a deleted file on the suspect's computer proves possession, and handling the question 'couldn't someone else have used this computer?'
setup_requirements: Mock trial scenario with forensic evidence
Mock courtroom setup (or classroom configured for exercise)
Expert witness CV template
Cross-examination question bank for digital forensics
Daubert challenge preparation guide

review.value: Testability: forensic imaging produces a hash that can be independently verified. Peer review: the methodology follows NIST SP 800-86, a peer-reviewed published standard. Error rate: SHA-256 has a collision probability of 1 in 2^128 (effectively zero). General acceptance: forensic imaging with hash verification is standard practice in every forensic laboratory worldwide.

Defense: 'Couldn't someone else have used this computer?' Answer honestly: 'My analysis shows that the files were created under the user account associated with the defendant, at times consistent with the defendant's known schedule, and I found no evidence of remote access or other user accounts. However, I cannot definitively exclude the possibility of another person using the computer.' This is honest and maintains credibility.

IP addresses: 'Like a phone number for a building, it identifies the connection, not the person.' Deleted files: 'Like tearing a page out of a book. The table of contents entry is removed but the page is still there until something new is written on it.' Hash values: 'Like a fingerprint for a file. If the fingerprints match, the files are identical.'

During cross-examination: if the attorney asks a loaded question, answer the factual component and ignore the implication. If asked to speculate, say 'I cannot speculate about that.' If asked about something outside your analysis, say 'That was outside the scope of my examination.' Calm, factual responses are more persuasive than defensive arguments.

learning_objectives: Conduct a complete cybercrime investigation from initial report through case file preparation
Apply all investigation techniques learned throughout the course in an integrated scenario
Produce a prosecution-ready case file with evidence, reports, and legal documentation
Present investigation findings in a professional briefing format
readings: All course materials review
FBI Cyber Investigation case file template
Prosecution memorandum examples

lecture_notes.introduction: The capstone investigation integrates every skill covered in this course into a single realistic scenario. You will receive a cybercrime report and must conduct the investigation from start to finish: identify the crime type and applicable statutes, collect and preserve digital evidence following forensic standards, analyze evidence across multiple sources (disk images, memory dumps, network captures, mobile devices, email headers, and social media), maintain chain of custody throughout, draft the necessary legal process (search warrants, preservation letters, provider requests), build the attribution chain from digital evidence to suspect identity, write a forensic examination report, and prepare a prosecution memorandum summarizing the case for an Assistant United States Attorney. This exercise simulates the workflow of a federal cybercrime investigation and tests your ability to apply technical, procedural, and legal skills together. The scenario involves multiple evidence sources that must be correlated, anti-forensic techniques that must be detected and addressed, cross-jurisdictional considerations, and evidence that supports alternative explanations that must be investigated and resolved. Your deliverable is a complete case file that a prosecutor could use to evaluate whether to bring charges.
key_concepts: Investigation workflow: report intake, preliminary assessment, evidence identification, legal process, evidence collection, forensic analysis, timeline construction, attribution, report writing, prosecution referral
Multi-source evidence correlation: linking findings across disk forensics, memory forensics, network forensics, mobile forensics, and open-source intelligence into a coherent narrative
