Anti-forensic detection and response: identifying timestomping, log deletion, encrypted containers, secure deletion, and evaluating how anti-forensic actions affect the evidence and what artifacts remain

Attribution chain construction: building the link from digital evidence (IP addresses, accounts, cryptocurrency) through investigative steps (provider records, financial records, OSINT) to a specific individual

Alternative hypothesis investigation: investigating reasonable alternative explanations for the evidence (someone else used the computer, the account was compromised, the evidence was planted) and documenting why they are inconsistent with the evidence

Prosecution memorandum: summarizing the investigation, evidence, and legal theory in a format that allows the AUSA to make a charging decision; identifying strengths, weaknesses, and additional investigation needs
Case file organization: organizing evidence, reports, legal process, and correspondence in a systematic structure that allows any team member to understand the case
Ethical considerations: ensuring the investigation stays within legal bounds, protecting the rights of the suspect, handling exculpatory evidence (Brady obligations), and maintaining objectivity
Team coordination: in real investigations, multiple agents and analysts work together; defining roles, sharing information securely, and maintaining consistent documentation standards
lecture_notes.demonstration: The instructor walks through a completed mock investigation to demonstrate the expected scope, quality, and organization of the capstone deliverable. Highlights include: how the initial report was triaged and the investigation plan was developed, how evidence from different sources was correlated to build the timeline, how anti-forensic techniques were detected and addressed, how the attribution chain was constructed step by step, and how the case file was organized for prosecution review.
setup_requirements: Capstone scenario package: cybercrime report, forensic images (disk and memory), packet captures, mobile device data, email headers, social media profiles
All forensic analysis tools from previous sessions
Legal process templates (warrant affidavit, preservation letter, provider request)
Case file template and prosecution memorandum template
Presentation equipment for final briefing

review.value: Before diving into forensic analysis, develop an investigation plan: identify the crime type and elements that must be proven, list the evidence sources to examine and their priority order, identify the legal process needed for each evidence source, and define the timeline for the investigation. The plan guides your work and ensures you do not miss critical evidence.

Create a matrix mapping findings to evidence sources. If disk forensics shows a file was created at 10:15 PM, network forensics shows data uploaded at 10:20 PM, and the suspect's phone location shows them at home at that time, the correlation strengthens each individual finding. Uncorroborated findings are weaker.

Under Brady v. Maryland, the prosecution must disclose evidence favorable to the defense. As an investigator, if you find evidence that suggests the suspect may be innocent (an alibi in location data, evidence of account compromise suggesting someone else used the account), you must document and report it. Hiding exculpatory evidence is both unethical and illegal.

A well-organized case file allows the case to survive personnel changes. If you transfer to another unit, retire, or are unavailable for trial, another investigator must be able to pick up the file and understand the entire case. Organize by evidence type, index all exhibits, and include a case summary that provides the narrative without requiring the reader to examine every document.

quizzes.topic: Cybercrime Typology, Digital Evidence, and Chain of Custody

questions.question: During a cybercrime investigation, you arrive at a suspect's office and find a Windows laptop that is powered on and logged in. Describe, in order, the specific steps you would take before, during, and after powering off the laptop, explaining the forensic rationale for each step.
questions.answer: Before powering off: (1) Photograph the screen to capture the current state (open windows, running applications, taskbar). (2) Document the system time and compare to actual time for clock skew analysis. (3) Capture volatile memory using WinPMEM or DumpIt to a forensically prepared USB drive. RAM contains encryption keys, running processes, network connections, and data that exists only in memory. (4) Run a live network connection capture (netstat output) to document active connections that will be lost. (5) If full-disk encryption is detected (BitLocker icon), the system must remain powered to preserve access to decrypted data. Perform a live logical acquisition of the file system before powering off. During power-off: (6) If encryption is not a concern, pull the power plug rather than performing a clean shutdown. A clean shutdown may trigger anti-forensic scripts, encrypt data, or overwrite evidence. Pulling the plug preserves the disk state at that exact moment. (7) If BitLocker recovery key is available, a clean shutdown is acceptable since the key allows future decryption. After powering off: (8) Remove the hard drive and photograph it (model, serial number). (9) Connect through a hardware write-blocker and create a forensic image (dd or E01 format). (10) Calculate SHA-256 hash of both source and image, verify they match. (11) Package the laptop in a tamper-evident evidence bag with evidence tag. (12) Complete the chain of custody form documenting all steps, times, and personnel. Rationale: the order (memory first, then disk) follows the order of volatility. Memory is destroyed at power-off; disk persists.

rubric.key: Correct volatile evidence capture before power-off (memory, network connections, screen state)
Proper handling of encryption considerations affecting power-off decision
Correct post-power-off imaging procedure with write-blocker and hash verification

questions.question: A defense attorney challenges the admissibility of a forensic image, arguing that MD5 hash verification is unreliable because MD5 collisions are known to be possible. How would you respond, and what steps should have been taken to prevent this challenge?
questions.answer: Response to the challenge: MD5 collision attacks produce two different inputs with the same hash, but they require the attacker to control both inputs. In forensic imaging, the input (the evidence drive) is fixed; the question is whether the image matches the original. An attacker cannot produce a modified image that has the same MD5 hash as the original without controlling the original drive's contents. Therefore, MD5 collision attacks do not undermine forensic image verification in this context. However, the concern is valid in principle because MD5 is a deprecated algorithm. Prevention steps that should have been taken: (1) Use SHA-256 as the primary verification hash, not MD5. SHA-256 has no known collision attacks and is the current standard. (2) Compute both MD5 and SHA-256 at acquisition time for backward compatibility with older case management systems. (3) Document in the acquisition report that SHA-256 was the primary verification method. (4) Re-verify the SHA-256 hash before analysis and before testimony. (5) If the defense expert produces a colliding MD5 input, the SHA-256 values will differ, proving the images are not the same. Best practice: always use SHA-256 as the primary hash. Include MD5 as a secondary hash for compatibility. Document the choice of algorithm and its collision resistance properties in the examination report.

rubric.key: Correct technical explanation of why MD5 collision attacks do not directly undermine forensic imaging
Recognition that SHA-256 should be the primary hash and MD5 is deprecated
Preventive measures including dual-hash verification and documentation

questions.question: Classify each of the following as computer-as-target, computer-as-tool, or computer-as-incidental, and identify the primary federal statute for each: (a) DDoS attack on a bank's website, (b) wire fraud conducted through email, (c) drug trafficking where the dealer uses encrypted messaging to coordinate sales.
questions.answer: (a) DDoS attack on a bank's website: Computer-as-target. The computer system (the bank's web server) is the direct target of the attack. The crime is the unauthorized impairment of the computer system's availability. Primary statute: 18 USC 1030(a)(5) - knowingly causing damage to a protected computer. This subsection covers intentional damage through transmission of code, commands, or information. The bank's website is a 'protected computer' under 1030(e)(2) because it is used in interstate commerce. (b) Wire fraud through email: Computer-as-tool. The computer (email system) is the instrument used to commit the fraud, but the crime is the fraud itself. Primary statute: 18 USC 1343 - wire fraud. The elements are: (1) a scheme to defraud, (2) material misrepresentation, (3) use of interstate wire communications (email) in furtherance of the scheme. CFAA (1030) may also apply if the attacker gained unauthorized access to the email system. (c) Drug trafficking with encrypted messaging: Computer-as-incidental. The crime is drug trafficking; the computer (phone with encrypted messaging) is incidental to the crime, holding evidence of the criminal activity. Primary statute: 21 USC 841 - drug distribution. The encrypted messages are evidence of the trafficking conspiracy. The computer crime statutes (1030) do not apply because no unauthorized computer access occurred. The messages may be obtained via device seizure (warrant) or provider records (if available).

rubric.key: Correct classification of all three scenarios
Correct primary federal statute for each with section reference
Explanation of why each classification applies

quizzes.topic: Forensic Imaging, File System Analysis, and Windows Artifacts

questions.question: You are analyzing an NTFS forensic image and find a file with the following timestamps: $STANDARD_INFORMATION created: 2022-01-15, modified: 2022-03-20; $FILE_NAME created: 2025-11-30, modified: 2025-11-30. What does this discrepancy indicate, what tool was likely used, and how would you report this finding?
questions.answer: This discrepancy indicates timestomping: the deliberate manipulation of file timestamps to deceive investigators. The $FILE_NAME (FN) timestamps show the file was actually created on 2025-11-30. The $STANDARD_INFORMATION (SI) timestamps were then modified to show 2022-01-15, making the file appear to have been created nearly four years earlier. The attacker's goal was likely to make the file appear to predate the period under investigation, so it would be excluded from time-based searches. The tool likely used was Timestomp (from the Metasploit framework), SetMACE, or a similar utility that modifies SI timestamps through user-space Windows API calls (SetFileTime). These tools modify SI timestamps but cannot modify FN timestamps because FN timestamps are stored in the MFT directory index and can only be modified by the NTFS driver at the kernel level. Report finding: 'Analysis of file [filename] revealed a discrepancy between $STANDARD_INFORMATION and $FILE_NAME timestamps. The FN timestamps (created 2025-11-30) are consistent with the file being created on that date. The SI timestamps (created 2022-01-15) appear to have been deliberately modified using a timestamp manipulation tool. The FN timestamps are considered more reliable because they require kernel-level access to modify. This finding indicates the user or an application on this system took deliberate steps to alter the apparent creation date of this file, which is consistent with anti-forensic activity.'

rubric.key: Correct identification of timestomping with explanation of SI vs FN timestamp modification levels
Identification of likely tools and technique explanation
Professional report language with appropriate forensic conclusions

questions.question: Describe three distinct types of information that can be recovered from NTFS unallocated space and explain how each would be relevant in a data theft investigation.
questions.answer: Type 1: Deleted file contents. When a file is deleted in NTFS, the MFT entry is marked as available and the data clusters are marked as unallocated, but the actual data remains on disk until overwritten by new data. Relevance: If the suspect downloaded stolen data, processed it, and then deleted the files, the deleted data may be fully recoverable from unallocated space. File carving tools (Scalpel, PhotoRec) can identify file signatures and reconstruct complete files even without MFT entries. This proves the suspect had the stolen data on their system even though it no longer appears in the active file system. Type 2: File slack space data. Each file occupies whole clusters, but the file data may not fill the last cluster completely. The space between the end of file data and the end of the cluster (file slack) may contain data from a previously deleted file that occupied those same sectors. Relevance: Fragments of deleted documents, spreadsheets, or communications in file slack can provide evidence of data handling activities that the suspect attempted to hide. Even partial document recovery can reveal customer names, account numbers, or proprietary data. Type 3: Deleted MFT entries. When a file is deleted and its MFT entry is later reused, the original metadata (filename, timestamps, size, parent directory) may be partially overwritten but fragments of the previous entry may remain in the MFT slack space. Relevance: Recovering deleted MFT entries reveals the original filename, directory location, creation timestamp, and file size even when the file data has been overwritten. Knowing that a file named 'customer_database_export.csv' (1.2 GB) existed in the suspect's Downloads folder on a specific date is valuable evidence even without the file contents.

rubric.key: Three distinct recovery types correctly described with NTFS technical details
Clear relevance explanation for each type in the data theft context
Technically accurate description of how NTFS deletion works

questions.question: You find that Windows Event Log ID 1102 (audit log cleared) was generated at 2:00 AM on the date of a suspected intrusion. The Security event log now starts at 2:00 AM with no earlier entries. What investigative steps would you take to recover the lost log data, and what can you infer from Event 1102 itself?
questions.answer: Inferences from Event 1102: The event records the account that cleared the log (subject account name and SID), the timestamp of the clearing, and the logon ID of the session that performed the clearing. This tells us who cleared the log (or which compromised account was used) and exactly when. Log clearing is itself evidence of consciousness of guilt. Recovery steps: (1) Check for event log forwarding. If the organization uses Windows Event Forwarding (WEF) or a SIEM (Splunk, Elastic), the original events may exist on the collection server even though they were cleared locally. (2) Examine Volume Shadow Copies. If VSS snapshots exist from before 2:00 AM, the Security.evtx file in the shadow copy contains the events that were cleared. Use vssadmin or Arsenal Image Mounter to access shadow copies. (3) Analyze the NTFS $LogFile and $UsnJrnl. These file system journals may contain records of the event log file being modified or truncated, providing additional timestamps and evidence of the clearing activity. (4) Check other event logs that were not cleared. The System log and Application log may contain related events (service starts, errors) that correlate with the attacker's activity. (5) Examine the event log file itself. When the log is cleared, the old .evtx file may have been backed up or the data blocks may exist in unallocated space. Carve for .evtx file signatures in unallocated space. (6) Check for Sysmon logs. If Sysmon was installed, it maintains a separate log that may not have been cleared. (7) Cross-reference with network logs (firewall, proxy, IDS) which are external to the compromised system and likely not cleared by the attacker.

rubric.key: Correct inferences from Event 1102 including captured account information
Multiple recovery methods described (SIEM, VSS, file system journals)
Recognition of external log sources that survive local log clearing

quizzes.topic: Memory Forensics, Network Forensics, and Mobile Forensics

questions.question: Using Volatility 3, you analyze a memory dump and find that svchost.exe (PID 1234) has an active TCP connection to IP 203.0.113.50 on port 443, contains executable code regions not mapped to any DLL on disk, and its parent process is cmd.exe rather than services.exe. Explain each anomaly and what they collectively indicate.
questions.answer: Anomaly 1: Parent process is cmd.exe instead of services.exe. Legitimate svchost.exe processes are always spawned by services.exe (the Service Control Manager). A svchost.exe spawned by cmd.exe indicates the process is not a real Windows service but was launched manually or by malware. This is a strong indicator of malicious activity. Anomaly 2: Executable code regions not mapped to any DLL on disk. Volatility's malfind plugin detects executable memory regions that do not correspond to any loaded DLL or the main executable. This indicates code injection: malicious code was injected into the svchost.exe process memory space. Common techniques include process hollowing (replacing the legitimate code), DLL injection (loading a malicious DLL), and reflective DLL loading (loading a DLL entirely in memory without touching disk). Anomaly 3: Active TCP connection to external IP on port 443. While svchost.exe can legitimately make network connections (Windows Update, etc.), a connection to an unknown external IP combined with the other anomalies indicates command and control (C2) communication. Port 443 is used to disguise the traffic as legitimate HTTPS. Collective interpretation: This is a compromised system with an active backdoor. An attacker injected malicious code into a svchost.exe process (chosen because it is a common system process that will not attract attention), and the injected code is communicating with a C2 server at 203.0.113.50:443. The use of process injection (fileless technique) means the malware exists only in memory and will not be found by traditional disk forensics. Investigation next steps: extract the injected code from memory for malware analysis, investigate the C2 IP through WHOIS and threat intelligence, and check for lateral movement to other systems.

rubric.key: Correct analysis of each individual anomaly with technical explanation
Sound collective interpretation indicating compromised system with active C2
Appropriate next investigation steps

questions.question: Network forensic analysis reveals DNS queries from an internal host to suspicious.evil.com with subdomain labels averaging 60 characters of base64-encoded data, occurring every 30 seconds. The TXT record responses also contain base64 data. Identify the attack technique, explain how it works, and describe how you would investigate it.
questions.answer: Attack technique: DNS tunneling for data exfiltration and C2 communication. How it works: DNS tunneling abuses the DNS protocol to create a covert communication channel. Data to be exfiltrated is encoded (base64) and placed in DNS query subdomain labels: [base64-encoded-data].suspicious.evil.com. The attacker controls the authoritative DNS server for evil.com, which receives the queries, decodes the data, and responds with encoded instructions in TXT records. The technique works because: (1) DNS is almost always allowed through firewalls (port 53 is rarely blocked). (2) DNS queries appear as normal name resolution activity. (3) The data is carried in the query itself, not requiring direct connectivity to the attacker's server. The 30-second interval indicates automated beaconing rather than manual interaction. Investigation steps: (1) Identify the internal host: correlate the DNS queries with the source IP in DNS server logs or in the network capture. (2) Isolate the host: disconnect from the network to prevent further data exfiltration while preserving the running state for memory forensics. (3) Capture memory: the DNS tunneling client in memory will reveal the C2 protocol and decryption keys. (4) Decode the exfiltrated data: base64-decode the subdomain labels to determine what data was transmitted. Reconstruct the full data stream by ordering queries by timestamp. (5) Investigate the C2 domain: WHOIS registration for suspicious.evil.com, DNS server hosting, and threat intelligence lookup. (6) Quantify the exfiltration: DNS labels can carry approximately 180 bytes per query. At 30-second intervals, that is approximately 518 KB per day, or 3.6 MB per week, enough to exfiltrate credentials, documents, and database exports over time. (7) Assess scope: check DNS logs for the same query pattern from other internal hosts to determine if the compromise is isolated or widespread.

rubric.key: Correct identification and technical explanation of DNS tunneling
Comprehensive investigation steps from host identification through scope assessment
Data exfiltration quantification and impact assessment

questions.question: You receive a locked iPhone (passcode unknown) that is a suspect's personal device in a fraud investigation. Describe the legal and technical options available, including what data might be obtainable from Apple through legal process versus what requires device access.
questions.answer: Legal options for device access: (1) Consent: ask the suspect to unlock the device voluntarily. Document consent in writing. Consent can be revoked at any time. (2) Search warrant with compelled unlock: a warrant can authorize seizure but compelling the suspect to provide the passcode raises Fifth Amendment issues. Courts are split: some treat providing a passcode as testimonial (protected), while biometric unlock (fingerprint, face) is generally considered non-testimonial (compellable). (3) Search warrant with technical bypass: obtain a warrant authorizing forensic access and use technical tools (GrayKey, UFED Premium) if they support the specific iPhone model and iOS version. Success depends on the device generation and whether USB Restricted Mode has engaged (1 hour without unlock). Technical options: (4) Cellebrite UFED Premium or GrayShift GrayKey: commercial tools that exploit vulnerabilities in specific iOS versions to bypass the passcode. Effectiveness varies by device model and iOS version. (5) checkm8 exploit: for older iPhones (iPhone 5S through iPhone X), a hardware-based bootloader exploit allows file system extraction. Not available for newer models. (6) iTunes backup extraction: if the device has been backed up to a computer and the backup is unencrypted, the backup data can be analyzed without device access. Data obtainable from Apple via legal process: (7) With a warrant: iCloud backup contents (if enabled), iCloud Drive files, iCloud Photos, Mail, Notes, Contacts, Calendars, Safari bookmarks, iMessage (if stored in iCloud). (8) With a court order: account registration information, connection logs with IP addresses and timestamps, iCloud Mail logs. (9) With a subpoena: basic subscriber information (name, address, email, phone number associated with the Apple ID). Limitation: Apple cannot decrypt end-to-end encrypted data (Messages in iCloud with Advanced Data Protection, Health data, Keychain). If the suspect enabled Advanced Data Protection, most iCloud data is end-to-end encrypted and Apple cannot produce it.

rubric.key: Legal analysis of compelled unlock including Fifth Amendment considerations
Technical bypass options with model/version limitations acknowledged
Accurate description of data obtainable from Apple at each legal threshold

quizzes.topic: Email Investigation, Dark Web, and Legal Procedures

questions.question: During a BEC investigation, you receive the fraudulent email's full headers showing it passed SPF checks for the domain 'trusted-bank.com', but the Reply-To address is 'accounting@trusted-bannk.com' (note: two n's). Explain the attack technique, what the SPF pass means, and your investigation steps for the lookalike domain.
questions.answer: Attack technique: This is a BEC attack using a lookalike (homograph) domain combined with Reply-To manipulation. The attacker registered trusted-bannk.com (two n's) which visually resembles trusted-bank.com. The email was actually sent from trusted-bannk.com's mail servers, which is why SPF passed: the SPF record for trusted-bannk.com authorizes those servers to send email for that domain. The victim sees 'trusted-bank.com' in the display name but if they reply, the response goes to the attacker's lookalike domain. SPF pass interpretation: SPF passing means the email was sent from a server authorized by the sending domain's DNS SPF record. It does NOT mean the email is from the legitimate organization. SPF only validates the domain in the envelope sender, and if the attacker controls the lookalike domain, they control the SPF record. This is a common misunderstanding: SPF pass does not equal legitimacy when the domain itself is malicious. Investigation steps: (1) WHOIS lookup on trusted-bannk.com: registration date (likely recent, days before the attack), registrant information (likely privacy-protected), registrar, and name servers. (2) DNS analysis: MX records, A records, and hosting provider for the domain. Subpoena the registrar for registration records including payment information, original registrant email, and IP address used during registration. (3) Web presence check: does trusted-bannk.com have a website? If so, it may be a cloned version of the legitimate site, and the hosting provider records may reveal the attacker. (4) Historical DNS: check passive DNS databases for the domain's resolution history. (5) Certificate transparency logs: check if any SSL certificates were issued for the domain, which may reveal additional infrastructure. (6) Reply analysis: if the victim replied, the response went to the attacker's mail server. Subpoena the hosting/mail provider for the lookalike domain to obtain access logs, stored emails, and other account data. (7) Cross-reference: check if trusted-bannk.com appears in threat intelligence databases or has been used in other attacks.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of lookalike domain attack and Reply-To manipulation
Accurate explanation of why SPF passed and what it does not guarantee
Comprehensive investigation steps for the lookalike domain

questions.question: You need to obtain email content from a suspect's Gmail account for a federal cybercrime investigation. Draft the legal process requirements: what legal authority is needed, what specific statute sections apply, what you must include in the warrant application, and what Google's process is for responding.
questions.answer: Legal authority required: A search warrant is needed for email content. Under the Stored Communications Act (18 USC 2703(a)), a warrant based on probable cause is required to compel a provider to disclose the contents of electronic communications in electronic storage. While 2703(b) historically allowed a subpoena for emails older than 180 days, the Sixth Circuit's Warshak decision (2010) and the subsequent Carpenter precedent mean courts now require a warrant for all email content regardless of age. Google's own policy requires a warrant for all content. Warrant application must include: (1) Probable cause establishing that the Gmail account contains evidence of the specified federal crime. (2) Particularity: specify the Gmail account (email address), the types of data sought (email content, contacts, Google Drive files, search history, YouTube history, Google Maps location history), date range limitations tied to the period of criminal activity, and keyword or account-based search terms. (3) The statutory authority: 18 USC 2703(a) for content, 18 USC 2703(c) for subscriber records. (4) The specific crimes under investigation with statutory citations. (5) Declaration that the warrant is served on Google LLC, located in the Northern District of California, pursuant to 18 USC 2703(a). Google's response process: (1) Service: submit the warrant through Google's Law Enforcement Request System (LERS) at lers.google.com or serve by mail/email to Google's Legal Investigations Support team. (2) Scope review: Google's legal team reviews the warrant for proper form and scope. They may push back on overbroad requests. (3) Production timeline: Google typically responds within 2-4 weeks for standard requests. Emergency requests (threat to life) receive expedited processing. (4) Format: Google produces data in a standardized format including email content (MBOX or PDF), account activity logs, and metadata. (5) Notification: Google may notify the account holder unless the warrant includes a non-disclosure order under 18 USC 2705(b). Additional steps: Send a preservation letter under 18 USC 2703(f) immediately to prevent data deletion during the warrant application process. Google will preserve the account data for 90 days (renewable).

rubric.key: Correct legal authority with specific SCA statute sections
Comprehensive warrant application requirements including particularity
Accurate description of Google's response process and preservation

questions.question: An investigator discovers a dark web vendor selling stolen credit card data on a Tor hidden service. The vendor accepts only Monero (privacy coin). Compare the investigation challenges of tracing Monero versus Bitcoin, and describe two alternative investigation approaches that do not rely on cryptocurrency tracing.
questions.answer: Bitcoin tracing: Bitcoin transactions are recorded on a public blockchain with transparent sender/receiver addresses and amounts. Chain analysis tools (Chainalysis, Elliptic) cluster addresses to entities, trace funds through multiple hops, and identify when funds reach regulated exchanges where KYC records link addresses to identities. Bitcoin mixers (CoinJoin, Wasabi Wallet) complicate tracing but are not always effective against advanced chain analysis. Monero tracing challenges: Monero uses three privacy technologies that make chain analysis extremely difficult: (1) Ring signatures hide the true sender among decoy inputs, making it unclear which input actually funded the transaction. (2) Stealth addresses generate a one-time address for each transaction, preventing address clustering. (3) Confidential transactions hide the transaction amount. Unlike Bitcoin, Monero's blockchain does not reveal sender, receiver, or amount. While academic research has identified potential weaknesses in older Monero versions, current Monero is considered effectively untraceable through blockchain analysis alone. Alternative investigation approaches: Approach 1: OPSEC failure analysis and undercover operations. Most dark web vendors eventually make operational security mistakes: reusing usernames, shipping from a consistent location (postal inspectors can identify origin), communicating in a distinctive writing style, or revealing personal details in private messages. An undercover agent purchasing from the vendor can establish a relationship, gather intelligence through private communications, and potentially arrange an in-person meeting or controlled delivery. Shipping physical goods creates a physical evidence trail (return addresses, shipping labels, fingerprints on packages, surveillance of shipping locations). Approach 2: Server-side investigation. If the hidden service has a vulnerability (misconfigured server revealing the real IP, CVE in the web application), investigators can identify the server's actual IP address. A warrant to the hosting provider reveals the subscriber and payment information. Alternatively, with proper authority (NIT warrant), investigators can deploy code on the hidden service to identify connecting users. The Welcome to Video case demonstrated that even Tor hidden services can be identified through technical investigation of the server infrastructure.

rubric.key: Accurate comparison of Bitcoin transparency vs Monero privacy technologies
First alternative approach well-developed with practical details
Second alternative approach well-developed with legal authority consideration

quizzes.topic: Report Writing, Expert Testimony, and Comprehensive Review

questions.question: You are testifying as an expert witness and the defense attorney asks: 'Isn't it true that your forensic tool, Autopsy, could have introduced errors into the evidence? Can you guarantee with 100% certainty that no artifacts were created by the tool itself?' How would you respond?
questions.answer: Response: 'No forensic tool can guarantee 100% accuracy in all circumstances, and I would not make such a claim. However, I can explain why the results from Autopsy are reliable in this case. Autopsy is an open-source forensic platform developed by Basis Technology and widely used by law enforcement agencies worldwide, including the FBI and Department of Defense. It has been subject to extensive peer review because its source code is publicly available for inspection. My analysis was performed on a verified forensic image, not the original evidence. The forensic image was verified by SHA-256 hash comparison at acquisition and again before analysis, confirming it is a bit-for-bit copy of the original. Autopsy reads the forensic image in a read-only manner and does not modify it. I can verify this by re-computing the hash of the image after analysis and showing it matches the acquisition hash. The specific findings I am testifying about - file existence, timestamps, and content - are based on the raw data stored in the NTFS file system structures, which I also verified by cross-referencing with a second tool (Sleuth Kit command-line tools) and obtained identical results. The consistency between two independent tools analyzing the same image provides additional confidence. Under the Daubert framework: the methodology is testable and has been tested, the tools are peer-reviewed and open-source, the error rate for hash verification is negligibly small, and the methodology is generally accepted throughout the digital forensics community.' Key principle: acknowledge the question honestly, do not claim perfection, but explain the safeguards that ensure reliability. Cross-tool verification is the strongest technical defense.

rubric.key: Honest acknowledgment without undermining credibility
Technical defense of tool reliability with specific safeguards (hash verification, read-only access)
Cross-tool verification and Daubert framework response

questions.question: Identify five specific deficiencies in the following forensic report excerpt and explain how each should be corrected: 'I examined the suspect's computer and found malware. The bad guy clearly downloaded it intentionally from a hacking website around February. The virus was really sophisticated and definitely from Russia based on the code comments. I used some tools to analyze it.'
questions.answer: Deficiency 1: 'suspect's computer' lacks specificity. Correction: Identify the evidence by exhibit number, make/model, serial number, and the forensic image identifier. Example: 'I examined forensic image CS-2025-001-HD1 (SHA-256: [hash]), created from a Dell Latitude 5520 laptop, serial number [SN], seized on [date], tagged as Exhibit 1.' Deficiency 2: 'The bad guy clearly downloaded it intentionally' is advocacy and speculation. The examiner is making a legal conclusion (intentional action) and using biased language ('bad guy'). Correction: 'The file [filename] was found at [path] with the following timestamps: [SI/FN dates]. Browser history shows a visit to [URL] at [timestamp]. The file's download location and browser history are consistent with the file being downloaded through the web browser.' Let the jury decide intent. Deficiency 3: 'from a hacking website around February' is vague and lacks evidence references. Correction: Identify the specific website URL, the specific date(s) from browser history or download records, and reference the evidence (Exhibit 1, browser history database at [path]). 'Around February' should be 'on February 12, 2025, at 14:32:07 UTC, as recorded in the Chrome History database (Exhibit 1, file path: Users/[username]/AppData/Local/Google/Chrome/User Data/Default/History).' Deficiency 4: 'definitely from Russia based on the code comments' overstates the conclusion. Code comments in Russian indicate the developer read/wrote Russian but do not prove Russian origin. Correction: 'Analysis of the executable revealed code comments in the Russian language. This is consistent with the malware having been developed by a Russian-speaking individual, but does not definitively establish the geographic origin of the malware.' Deficiency 5: 'I used some tools to analyze it' fails the methodology documentation standard. Correction: Specify every tool by name and version. 'Static analysis was performed using IDA Pro 8.3. Dynamic analysis was conducted in an isolated Windows 10 VM using Process Monitor v3.93 and Wireshark 4.2.3. The malware sample (SHA-256: [hash]) was detonated in the sandbox environment and network communications were captured for analysis.'

rubric.key: All five deficiencies correctly identified
Corrections demonstrate proper forensic report standards (specificity, objectivity, evidence references)
Corrected language is professional, precise, and admissible

questions.question: A prosecution memorandum must address both the strengths and weaknesses of the case. For a cybercrime case where the primary evidence is an IP address linking the suspect's home internet connection to the attack, describe three weaknesses the defense could exploit and how you would mitigate each.
questions.answer: Weakness 1: IP address identifies the connection, not the person. The defense will argue that anyone in the household (family members, guests) or anyone within Wi-Fi range could have used the connection. Mitigation: Corroborate the IP with additional evidence linking the specific individual to the activity. User account evidence (the activity occurred under the suspect's Windows user account), device evidence (the suspect's personal laptop was the source), timing evidence (the activity occurred during hours when the suspect was the only person home, established through other evidence), and behavioral evidence (browsing history on the suspect's device shows research related to the attack). Weakness 2: The IP could have been spoofed or the network compromised. The defense will suggest someone else used the suspect's Wi-Fi without authorization, or that the suspect's machine was compromised by a third party who used it as a proxy. Mitigation: Forensic analysis of the suspect's router for connected device logs, forensic analysis of the suspect's computer for remote access tools or malware that would support the 'compromised machine' theory. If no evidence of compromise is found, document that finding. Check for open Wi-Fi (no password) or WEP encryption (easily cracked). If the network used WPA2/WPA3 with a strong password, unauthorized access is unlikely. Weakness 3: DHCP and NAT mean the IP may have been reassigned. If the ISP uses dynamic IP allocation, the IP address at the time of the attack may have been assigned to a different customer. Mitigation: Obtain ISP records (via subpoena) confirming which customer account was assigned the specific IP address at the exact date and time of the attack. ISP DHCP lease logs map IP addresses to customer accounts with timestamps. Also obtain the customer's account information (name, address, payment method) to confirm the identity. Additionally, if the attack spanned multiple sessions, confirm the IP assignment for each session timestamp.

rubric.key: Three distinct and realistic defense weaknesses identified
Practical mitigation strategy for each weakness using corroborating evidence
Understanding that IP alone is insufficient and must be corroborated

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions, lab exercises, and the mock trial exercise. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement with the instructor.
course_policies.collaboration: Investigation discussions are encouraged. All case files, reports, and legal documents must be individual work. Sharing forensic findings between students is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Investigation exercises use simulated scenarios only. Conducting unauthorized investigations of real individuals, accessing systems without authorization, or using forensic tools outside the lab environment is illegal and grounds for expulsion.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop capable of running forensic analysis VMs. Forensic software (Autopsy, Volatility, Wireshark) is provided. Evidence media and forensic images are distributed through the course lab infrastructure.
